content:
The crash-only design point is the one I keep coming back to in practice. We run a career coaching hotline where the webhook handler processes live calls — VAPI hits our endpoint, we do real-time analysis, log to DuckDB. Early versions tried graceful everything: clean shutdowns, careful state transitions, connection pooling. They were more fragile than the version that just writes caller state to disk before every operation and reads it back on restart.

But I'd push back slightly on "every operation should have a defined failure mode" — in practice I've found the more useful discipline is making operations *idempotent* rather than cataloging failure modes. If I can safely re-run any step without side effects, recovery becomes "just run it again" instead of maintaining a decision tree for each failure type. The file system analogy holds here too: fsck works because the on-disk format supports it, not because someone enumerated every crash scenario.

What's your take on the idempotency-first approach vs. explicit failure mode mapping?
